entries.title: Target overview
entries.body:
# Target tool overview

**Target** is the "headquarters" of your test. Here Burp accumulates everything it has learned about the application, and here too you decide where exactly you're working and what mustn't be touched. Loosely, the tool has two roles:

- **A map of the application.** The **Site map** tab builds a tree of hosts, directories, and endpoints from all the traffic that passed through Burp. This is your model of the target: what exists, what methods, what parameters, where problems have already been found.
- **Test scope management.** The **Scope** tab sets the boundaries - which hosts and URLs are in scope. This affects what to display in the interface, what to log, what to intercept, and (in Professional) what to scan.

## Target tabs

| Tab | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Site map** | A tree of discovered content + request/response panels and issues |
| **Scope** | Include/exclude rules - what counts as "in scope" |
| **Issue definitions** | A reference of all the vulnerability types Burp Scanner can find |

## Two map modes: URL view and Crawl paths view

At the top of **Site map** there's a mode switch:

- **URL view** - the familiar hierarchical tree: root domain -> subdomains -> directories -> files. Suitable for most tasks.
- **Crawl paths view** - shows which paths the application actually takes to reach each resource (sequences of transitions). Useful for applications with state-dependent navigation, and for understanding how a user physically gets to a feature.

## A practical order of work

1. **Set the Scope first.** Before making any "noise", add the target host to scope - otherwise you'll drown in third-party traffic (CDN, analytics, widgets).
2. **Walk around the application through a browser.** Each request is passively logged into the **Site map**. Go through all the roles, all the sections, forms, file uploads.
3. **Analyze the attack surface.** Filter the tree, mark interesting points, look for parameters and dynamic URLs.
4. **Pass it on.** From the context menu of any node you can send a request to **Repeater**, **Intruder**, **Scanner** (Pro), **Organizer**, **Comparer**.

> Tip for bug bounty/CTF: Target is not a "check-the-box tab". A clean map and an accurate scope save hours: you see the entire attack surface in one place and don't scatter your effort across someone else's domains.

entries.title: Site map
entries.body:
# Site map

**Site map** is Burp's collected model of the target application: a tree of hosts, directories, and endpoints together with requests, responses, and the problems found. Essentially it's your "map of the terrain" for the whole test.

## Where the content comes from

The map is populated from several sources at once:

- **Passively from Proxy** - the main source. Everything you open in a browser with proxying enabled automatically appears in the tree. There's nothing separate to "launch".
- **Crawl** - the automatic crawl of the application parses responses, finds links and forms, and adds them to the map. Some of these links may not have been requested yet.
- **Content discovery / scanner results (Professional)** - the hidden directories and files found, the results of active and passive scanning.

## How to read the tree

In **URL view** the nodes are sorted alphabetically: root domain -> subdomains -> directories -> files. Pay attention to the color:

- **Black (bold) text** - the resource was actually requested, it has a response.
- **Gray text** - the resource is known (for example, a link was found during the crawl) but hasn't been requested yet. This is a hint that "it's worth going here".

Next to the methods (GET, POST, PUT, OPTIONS...) in Professional there may be colored circles - severity indicators of the issues found.

## The tab's panels

- **Content tree (left)** - the hierarchy of all the discovered resources.
- **Content table (top right)** - for the selected branch: URL, method, status code, length, MIME type, presence of parameters, notes. The columns are sortable and configurable.
- **Request / Response** - the full request-response pair for the selected item; the **Inspector** is available for convenient analysis of headers, parameters, and cookies.
- **Issues (Professional)** - the vulnerabilities found with a description, severity, and confidence.

## What to send on

Right-clicking a node or a table item opens the context menu. Typical actions:

- **Send to Repeater** - manual refinement of a specific request.
- **Send to Intruder** - automated brute-forcing/fuzzing of parameters.
- **Do passive/active scan** or **Scan** (Professional) - scanning the branch.
- **Add to scope / Remove from scope** - managing the scope right from the map.
- **Send to Organizer / Comparer** - save for analysis or compare two messages.

## Practice

- To quickly "saturate" the map, set the scope in advance and go through the application **by hand in all the roles** (anonymous, regular user, admin). The difference in available endpoints between roles is a gold mine for finding IDOR and access control violations.
- Gray (unrequested) nodes are a TODO list: go through them to get real responses.
- Comparing maps between runs helps you notice what appeared new after a role change or after a content discovery.

> If a node or endpoint didn't appear in the map - it means the traffic didn't pass through Burp. Check the browser's proxying and the HTTPS interception (the Burp certificate).

entries.title: Scope
entries.body:
# Scope

The **Target > Scope** tab tells Burp **which hosts and URLs you're testing**. This is, arguably, the first thing worth configuring in any project.

## Why scope is needed

- **Focus.** The filters for the tree and the proxy history can be switched to "in-scope only", and all the third-party noise vanishes from sight - CDN, telemetry, widgets, third-party APIs.
- **Clean traffic.** Burp Proxy can be configured to log and/or intercept **only in-scope** requests. The history becomes compact and readable.
- **Safety and legality.** The most important point. An accurate scope protects against accidentally scanning or attacking someone else's domains that you don't have permission for. In bug bounty, going outside the program is a rule violation; in a pentest - going beyond the contract.
- **Automation (Professional).** Live tasks can automatically scan only in-scope requests; Intruder and Repeater can follow redirects only within the scope.

## Two modes for defining the scope

### Simple mode (use simple scope)

A simple and fast way - a list of **URL prefixes** without regular expressions. It's enough to type a host or the start of a URL:

```
http://example.com/path
example.com
example.com:8080/login
```

If the protocol isn't specified, both HTTP and HTTPS match. There's an **Include subdomains** checkbox - it includes all of a host's subdomains (note: during scanning this can noticeably increase the volume of work).

### Advanced mode (advanced scope control)

Flexible rules based on matching across several fields at once. For a request to be considered in scope, **all** the specified fields of the rule must match:

| Field | Format |
|---|---|
| **Protocol** | HTTP, HTTPS, or any |
| **Host or IP range** | a regular expression or an IP range (for example, `10.1.1.1/24`) |
| **Port** | a regular expression for the port number |
| **File** | a regular expression for the path/file (the query string is ignored) |

Advanced mode is needed when simple prefixes aren't enough: for example, to include all subdomains of `*.target.com` but exclude `static.target.com`, or to limit yourself to a specific range of paths.

> Watch out for regex: a dot in a regex is "any character". To match exactly `example.com`, escape it: `example\.com`. Otherwise you can accidentally catch `exampleXcom` and the like.

## How to add rules

- **Add** - add a rule manually.
- **Paste URL** - paste a URL from the clipboard.
- **Load** - load a list from a text file (regex for the port and file is not supported when loading from a file).
- **Context menu** - on any request in Burp, select **Add to scope** or **Remove from scope**.

The rules are divided into **Include in scope** (what's in) and **Exclude from scope** (what we exclude). Exclude has priority: even if a host fell under include, a targeted exclude rule will remove it - handy for throwing out `/logout`, exit endpoints, or dangerous administrative actions.

## Practice

1. Add the main host to **Include** before starting any "noisy" actions.
2. When working with subdomains, use **Include subdomains** or the advanced rule `.*\.target\.com`.
3. Put everything destructive and noise-generating into **Exclude**: `/logout`, mass mailings, payment confirmations.
4. Enable the "in-scope only" filter in the map and the proxy history - working will become far more comfortable.

> When the **"Add item to scope?"** dialog appears during your first visit to a new host - this is Burp offering a convenient way to lock in the scope right away. Agree if the host really is yours.

entries.title: Filtering and searching the Site map
entries.body:
# Filtering and searching the Site map

The map of a real application quickly grows to thousands of nodes. The **map filter** controls what you see and helps you focus on what you need. Important: the filter **doesn't delete anything** - the hidden nodes will return as soon as you reset the filter.

The filter panel opens by clicking the filter bar above the tree.

## Filter categories

### By request type

- **Show only in-scope items** - only what's in the test scope (the most frequent mode).
- **Show only requested items** - only the resources actually requested (hide the gray, not-yet-requested ones).
- **Show only parameterized requests** - only requests with parameters; these are exactly the most interesting for finding injections.
- **Hide not-found items** - hide the 404s and other "junk".

### By response MIME type

Check only the content types you need: HTML, CSS, scripts, images, XML, JSON, etc. For example, when looking for API endpoints, leave JSON/XML and hide the images and styles.

### By status code

Show responses by code groups: `2xx`, `3xx`, `4xx`, `5xx`. Handy for catching, for example, all the `5xx` (potential application errors) or all the `3xx` (redirects).

### By file extension

Include (show only) or exclude (hide) specific extensions. A classic is to hide `.css`, `.png`, `.jpg`, `.woff` so the "functional" content remains.

### By folders

- **Hide empty folders** - hide directories that have no visible child items left after filtering. It cleans up the tree a lot.

### By annotations

Show items you've marked with a **note** or a **highlight**. This is a way to quickly return to points set aside "for later".

### Search by content (Professional)

The **Filter by search term** field searches the content of requests/responses:

- a literal string **or** a regular expression;
- a case-sensitivity checkbox (**case sensitive**);
- **negative search** - conversely, hide the matches.

## Searching for hidden content

The filter shows only what Burp already knows. To find **hidden** directories and files that aren't in the tree:

- use **Discover content** (an engagement tool, Professional) - it brute-forces directory/file names and recursively expands the map;
- pay attention to the **gray nodes** - these are links found during the crawl but not yet requested;
- look for hints in the code: comments, paths in JavaScript, mentions of endpoints (see the attack surface search tools).

## Practice

- A basic "combat" filter: **in-scope only** + **hide not-found** + hide static by extensions + **hide empty folders**. The tree becomes clean and manageable.
- For hunting injections, enable **show only parameterized requests** - all the input points are immediately visible.
- If you're looking for where a specific token/identifier/secret is mentioned - that's a search term by content (Pro).

> Remember: if something "disappeared" - you filtered it out, you didn't delete it. Resetting the filter will bring back the full picture.

entries.title: Issue definitions
entries.body:
# Issue definitions (a reference of vulnerability types)

The **Issue definitions** tab is a built-in reference of **all the problem types** that **Burp Scanner** can find. These aren't the results of your specific test, but rather an encyclopedia: what this vulnerability is, why it's dangerous, and how to fix it.

The reference is useful even without Professional/the scanner - as study material and as ready-made wording for reports.

## What's inside each definition

| Field | What it contains |
|---|---|
| **Description** | A detailed description of the essence of the vulnerability |
| **Remediation** | Concrete steps to fix it |
| **References** | Links to PortSwigger research and materials |
| **Vulnerability classifications** | A link to external classifications (for example, CWE) |
| **Typical severity** | The typical criticality: High / Medium / Low / Information |
| **Type index** | A hexadecimal identifier of the issue type |

## How to use it

- The list is sorted by the column headers - you can order it, for example, by severity and review the most critical classes first.
- Select an entry to see the full description.
- The **Open in browser** button opens the definition as a web page - handy for copying into a report.

Each real scanner finding corresponds to one of these types (that same **type index**), so from the issue itself in the test results it's easy to jump to its "theory".

## Practice

- **For reports.** The ready Description and Remediation blocks save time: there's no need to formulate from scratch every time what, say, reflected XSS is and how to close it. Take the meaning and rewrite it for the client's context.
- **For learning.** Reading the reference cover to cover is a quick way to refresh the taxonomy of web vulnerabilities: from injections and authentication problems to information leaks and minor information issues.
- **For prioritization.** The **Typical severity** field helps gauge what to look at first, but always assess the final criticality by the real impact in the specific application, not just by the "typical" label.

> Issue definitions are about classes of problems "in general". The vulnerabilities found in your case, with their own evidence, live in the scan results and in the Issues panel inside Site map.

entries.title: Manual mapping and attack surface analysis
entries.body:
# Manual mapping and attack surface analysis

The automatic crawl is good, but the **attack surface** is truly revealed through thoughtful manual mapping. Once the map is collected, the next step is to understand **where the application is most vulnerable** and to plan the test.

## Manual mapping

- **Walk through the application by hand** through a browser with proxying enabled - each request is passively logged into the **Site map**. This gives the most accurate map, including features that the automatic crawl doesn't find (multi-step forms, post-authentication actions, state-dependent content).
- **Go through all the roles.** Anonymous, regular user, privileged user, admin. The difference in available endpoints between roles is a direct path to IDOR and access control violations.
- **Don't forget the "invisible":** API endpoints, AJAX requests, WebSocket, hidden form fields, parameters in JS.

## Engagement tools (Professional)

From the context menu of a map branch, surface analysis tools are available:

- **Analyze target** - you select branches of the tree and get a summary of the attack surface: a list of **dynamic URLs** (with parameters), **static URLs**, and **all the parameters** that were encountered. This is a quick way to see the real input points in their entirety, rather than catching them one by one.
- **Discover content** - an active search for hidden directories and files; what's found is added to the map.
- **Search** - a search for an arbitrary string/regex across all requests and responses.
- **Find comments** - collects HTML/JS comments: forgotten endpoints, keys, TODOs, and debug notes often surface there.
- **Find scripts** - collects all the script references and inline JS for analyzing the client-side logic.
- **Find references** - shows where there are links to the selected resource (how it "is reached").

## What to look at first

When analyzing, prioritize by the "dangerous" signs:

- **Input points with parameters** - candidates for injections (SQLi, XSS, command injection, SSTI, etc.).
- **File upload** - potential type bypass, path traversal, RCE.
- **Authentication and password reset** - login logic, tokens, access recovery.
- **Object identifiers in parameters** (`id=`, `user=`, `account=`) - a check for IDOR.
- **Redirects and URL values in parameters** - open redirect, SSRF.
- **Non-standard/service endpoints** - `/admin`, `/api`, `/debug`, `/actuator`.

## Practice

- **Annotate as you go.** Mark interesting nodes with a highlight and notes - it's easy to return later via the annotations filter.
- **Mapping first, attack second.** A full surface map saves time: you won't pull the same endpoint twice and won't miss entire sections.
- **A combination of tools:** an interesting point from the map - into **Repeater** (manual refinement) or **Intruder** (brute-forcing). Add the endpoints found via Find comments/Find scripts to the map and run them too.

> A good model of the target is half the work. The more accurately you understand the attack surface, the fewer blind spots there are and the higher the chance of finding what the automatic scanners missed.
